text:
ONCOLOGY OFFICE VISIT NOTE -- URGENT FOLLOW-UP
Department of Medical Oncology

**PATIENT:** Marcus R. Ellison
**MRN:** [on file]
**DOB:** 1969
**ATTENDING PHYSICIAN:** [Attending Oncologist]
**VISIT TYPE:** Urgent follow-up -- confirmed radiographic progression on targeted therapy

---

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**

Mr. Ellison is a 56-year-old White male with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV (M1c) metastatic melanoma, presenting today for urgent reassessment following findings on his most recent CT imaging showing unequivocal disease progression on Tafinlar (Tafinlar) and tram (Mekinist). He was called back for this visit within 72 hours of the scan results becoming available. He presents accompanied by his wife.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Mr. Ellison was diagnosed approximately 8 months ago with Stage IV metastatic melanoma arising from the skin of the upper back. At diagnosis, disease burden was extensive: a 4.2 cm ulcerated nodular primary lesion (Breslow thickness 6.1 mm, Clark level V), bilateral axillary and left cervical nodal disease, a right axillary conglomerate mass measuring nearly 5 cm, multiple hepatic metastases (up to 3.1 cm, six lesions total), and disseminated subcutaneous satellite nodules across the trunk and extremities. LDH at diagnosis was 642 U/L, approximately twice the upper limit of normal. BRAF V600E mutation was confirmed on both immunohistochemistry (VE1 antibody, strongly positive) and next-generation sequencing (allele frequency 48.2%). Germline testing was not considered clinically necessary given somatic VAF pattern.

Given the high disease burden, hepatic involvement, and markedly elevated LDH -- all factors that historically favor a rapid-response strategy -- the multidisciplinary tumor board recommended first-line BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy with Tafinlar 150 mg BID and tram 2 mg daily, initiated approximately 8 months ago.

Initial response was gratifying and consistent with what one expects from this regimen in BRAF V600E-mutant disease. At the 6-week clinical assessment, LDH had dropped to 214 U/L, palpable subcutaneous nodules had nearly completely resolved, the right axillary mass was substantially smaller, and the patient's performance status had improved from ECOG 1 to ECOG 0. CT restaging at approximately 3 months confirmed a robust partial response: the right axillary mass down from 4.8 to 1.9 cm, the dominant hepatic lesion (segment VII) down from 3.1 to 1.1 cm, and LDH normalized to 167 U/L. The 6-month CT showed continued partial response at nadir with stable residual disease and no new lesions.

However, his most recent CT scan (approximately 8 months from treatment start) now demonstrates unambiguous disease progression. The right axillary mass has grown from 1.7 cm back to 3.4 cm. The segment VII hepatic lesion has increased from 1.0 to 2.8 cm. Two new hepatic lesions have appeared -- one in segment IV (1.3 cm) and one in segment VI (0.9 cm). There are new subcutaneous nodules on the back and abdomen (largest 1.7 cm), and a new right external iliac lymph node measuring 1.2 cm. Notably absent, however, is any CNS involvement -- the brain was clear on MRI at baseline and there are no symptoms to suggest interval intracranial disease. LDH has again risen sharply to 589 U/L, nearly back to the baseline level.

Mr. Ellison reports he has noticed over the past 3-4 weeks that some of the bumps on his back and abdomen have reappeared and seem to be enlarging. He has also experienced some right upper quadrant fullness and mild nausea, which he attributes to the hepatic lesion growth. He reports worsening fatigue -- now more than the mild fatigue he had tolerated on dabra/Mekinist during the good response period. He denies any new neurological symptoms: no headaches, no focal weakness, no visual changes, no gait disturbance. No fevers, no weight loss beyond what has been present chronically, no jaundice, no change in bowel habits.

He was previously treated for a cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cuSCC) of the right forearm, identified at approximately 4 months into BRAF inhibitor therapy -- a well-recognized class effect of BRAF inhibitors from paradoxical MAPK activation in RAS-mutant keratinocytes. That was managed with wide local excision with clear margins and has not recurred. He has been followed by dermatology with monthly skin surveillance.

Mr. Ellison and his wife are understandably anxious about the progression findings but remain engaged and express a strong desire to continue aggressive treatment. He completed his landscaping work on a light-duty basis throughout treatment and is hoping to continue working as long as possible.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**

- Constitutional: Increased fatigue over the past 3-4 weeks (worse than prior baseline on therapy), mild decrease in appetite; no fever, no night sweats, no significant weight loss.
- HEENT: No headaches, no vision changes, no hearing changes.
- Neurological: No focal neurological deficits, no cognitive changes, no speech changes.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no dyspnea on exertion.
- Pulmonary: No cough, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea at rest or on exertion.
- Gastrointestinal: Mild right upper quadrant fullness and intermittent nausea; no vomiting, no diarrhea, no constipation, no melena, no hematochezia.
- Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria, no frequency.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized myalgia, which may relate to disease activity; no bone pain.
- Skin: Patient has noticed new subcutaneous nodules emerging on upper back and right lateral abdomen over the past 3-4 weeks. Existing cuSCC excision site on right forearm healing well per dermatology.
- Endocrine: No symptoms of thyroid dysfunction, no polyuria/polydipsia.
- Hematologic/lymphatic: Patient has noted renewed firmness in the right axillary region.
- Psychiatric: Situational anxiety related to progression news; denies depression, adequate sleep.

---

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY**
(Established at time of initial consult; no significant interim changes)
- Metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, Stage IV M1c -- as above
- Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, right forearm (4 months prior, treated with WLE, clear margins)
- Former smoker (quit 12 years ago; 10 pack-year history)
- No prior cardiac disease, no prior autoimmune conditions
- No prior malignancy other than melanoma and the iatrogenic cuSCC

**SURGICAL HISTORY**
- Wide local excision of right forearm cuSCC (4 months ago)
- No prior melanoma-directed surgeries (primary tumor not fully excised given M1c stage at presentation)

**FAMILY HISTORY**
- Maternal aunt: melanoma diagnosed in her 60s
- No other family history of melanoma or other cancers reported

**SOCIAL HISTORY**
- Occupation: Landscaper (returning to modified light-duty work over past several months)
- Lives with wife; two adult children
- Former smoker, quit 12 years ago, 10 pack-year history
- No significant alcohol use
- No illicit drug use
- Has maintained good social support system through treatment course

**ALLERGIES**
- NKDA (no known drug allergies)

**MEDICATIONS**
- Tafinlar (Tafinlar) 150 mg PO BID (held pending today's assessment)
- tram (Mekinist) 2 mg PO daily (held pending today's assessment)
- Acetaminophen 500 mg PO PRN (used occasionally for breakthrough pyrexia during BRAF/MEK therapy)
- Sunscreen SPF 50+ daily (per oncology/dermatology recommendation)
- Multivitamin daily

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION**

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-nourished, well-groomed male appearing stated age. Mild affect of anxiety; wife present and engaged in discussion. Not in acute distress.

Vital Signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm regular, RR 16 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Temperature 37.0°C, Weight 84 kg (stable from last visit).

Performance Status: ECOG 1 (some fatigue, otherwise ambulatory and capable of self-care).

Head and Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. No mucosal lesions. Left cervical level II/III region: palpable lymph node approximately 1.5 cm, firm, non-tender (previously noted to have markedly reduced during response period, now appears to have modestly re-enlarged). No supraclavicular adenopathy. No parotid masses.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, no crackles, no dullness to percussion.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No S3/S4 gallop. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft. Right upper quadrant mild tenderness on deep palpation without peritoneal signs. No frank guarding. Liver edge palpable approximately 2-3 cm below the right costal margin, mildly tender -- consistent with hepatic tumor involvement. No splenomegaly on examination. No ascites.

Axillary/Lymph Nodes: Right axilla: firm, irregular mass palpable in the right axillary region, estimated 3-4 cm, consistent with relapsed right axillary nodal mass seen on CT. Left axilla: no discrete mass. Right inguinal: no adenopathy. Left inguinal: no adenopathy.

Skin/Subcutaneous: 
- Multiple firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules palpable on the upper back (2-3 discrete lesions, largest approximately 1.5-1.8 cm) and right lateral abdominal wall (2 discrete nodules, largest approximately 1.5 cm) -- representing newly re-emerged/progressive subcutaneous disease.
- Healing scar at right forearm (cuSCC excision site); no recurrence palpated.
- Primary melanoma surgical excision site on upper back: no concerning findings at primary site.
- Grade 1 hyperkeratosis on bilateral palmar surfaces, previously noted, stable.
- No new suspicious pigmented lesions on survey exam (dermatology maintaining surveillance).

Neurological: Alert, oriented x3, intact cranial nerves II-XII. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation grossly intact. Coordination intact. Gait normal.

Extremities: No clubbing. No cyanosis. Trace ankle edema bilaterally (likely positional, not concerning for obstruction).

---

**LABORATORY RESULTS**
(Drawn today)

- LDH: 589 U/L (markedly elevated, ~2x ULN; mirrors pre-treatment level, alarming recapitulation of baseline burden)
- AST: 94 U/L (mildly elevated, likely hepatic metastatic involvement)
- ALT: 78 U/L (mildly elevated)
- Total bilirubin: 1.1 mg/dL (normal upper limit)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 112 U/L (mildly elevated)
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL (marginally low but acceptable)
- CBC: WBC 7.2 x10^9/L, Hgb 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, grade 1), Plt 188 x10^9/L
- Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (normal)
- TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (normal)
- Glucose: 98 mg/dL (normal; no hyperglycemia on dabra/Mekinist)
- Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1 (normal)

---

**IMAGING RESULTS REVIEWED**

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (third restaging scan, results reviewed in full today):
Compared to the 6-month scan, there is unequivocal progression. The right axillary nodal mass has roughly doubled, now back up to 3.4 cm from the 1.7 cm nadir. The dominant hepatic lesion in segment VII has nearly tripled, going from 1.0 cm back up to 2.8 cm. The segment II hepatic lesion is now 1.6 cm, back near its pretreatment size. Two new hepatic lesions are present in segments IV and VI. New subcutaneous nodules on the back and abdomen are visible, with the largest at 1.7 cm. There is a new right external iliac lymph node at 1.2 cm, a region that was clean on prior imaging. No pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusions. No new bone lesions. No ascites.

MRI Brain with and without gadolinium (ordered today, results pending -- will review prior to immunotherapy initiation).

---

**ASSESSMENT**

Mr. Ellison is a 56-year-old male with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV M1c melanoma with unambiguous disease progression on first-line dabra/Mekinist combination targeted therapy after achieving an initial excellent partial response lasting approximately 6-7 months. His clinical trajectory is unfortunately typical of what we see with BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations -- most patients derive meaningful benefit, often with dramatic early tumor shrinkage, but acquired resistance emerges in the majority, typically within 6-12 months.

I spent considerable time today with Mr. Ellison and his wife explaining the biology of acquired BRAF inhibitor resistance. Resistance mechanisms are varied but frequently include BRAF gene amplification (which effectively overwhelms the drug's inhibitory capacity), acquisition of MEK1/2 mutations that impair drug binding to the kinase domain, NRAS mutations that reactivate the MAPK pathway upstream of BRAF, or activation of parallel survival pathways such as PI3K/AKT signaling. Importantly, the tumor has typically retained its BRAF V600E mutation, but these additional acquired changes render the BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination ineffective. I have requested a re-biopsy of one of the new subcutaneous nodules on the abdomen to confirm the histological diagnosis (not in doubt clinically, but appropriate for tissue banking and resistance profiling), assess Ki-67 and mitotic activity, and send for repeat molecular profiling to characterize the specific acquired resistance mechanism. Understanding the molecular landscape at progression may also inform whether there is any actionable target beyond what is already known, though in the current setting, the most appropriate next step remains immunotherapy.

His LFTs are mildly elevated (AST 94, ALT 78), which likely reflects hepatic tumor burden rather than drug toxicity at this point (dabra and Mekinist are being held as of today). His bilirubin remains normal and albumin is preserved, so he is not in hepatic decompensation. I will recheck LFTs in 2 weeks and prior to initiating immunotherapy.

The most appropriate second-line treatment for BRAF-mutant melanoma that has progressed on targeted therapy is combination checkpoint immunotherapy. The CheckMate 067 regimen -- ipi (Yervoy) 3 mg/kg plus Opdivo (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivo maintenance -- remains the standard of care and is what I am recommending. PD-L1 expression was assessed at diagnosis (TPS 25%, CPS 38 by 28-8 assay), and TMB was intermediate-high at 18.4 mutations/megabase, both reasonably favorable prognostic features for immunotherapy. Notably, the tumor was MSS, which is the rule for melanoma; this does not detract from immunotherapy benefit in melanoma the way it does in colorectal cancer.

Before initiating immunotherapy, I have ordered an MRI of the brain to rule out interval intracranial metastatic disease, as the approach to CNS lesions may change immediate management. I also ordered baseline endocrine labs (AM cortisol, ACTH stimulation test, TSH/free T4, testosterone/gonadotropins) per our pre-immunotherapy baseline protocol. CBC, CMP, and LFTs are being repeated today to establish a pre-treatment laboratory baseline for immune-related adverse event surveillance.

I discussed with Mr. Ellison and his wife in detail the expected side effect profile of ipi plus Opdivo. The combination carries a substantially higher rate of immune-mediated adverse events than either agent alone. Approximately 50-60% of patients will experience some immune-mediated toxicity; grade 3-4 events occur in roughly 55-60%. The most clinically significant include immune-mediated colitis (diarrhea management counseling provided today with a written symptom diary), hepatitis (LFT surveillance), pneumonitis (dyspnea and cough monitoring), endocrinopathies (thyroiditis, hypophysitis, adrenal insufficiency), and dermatitis. I provided the patient and his wife with the clinic's immune toxicity management guide, emergency contact numbers, and clear instructions to present to the emergency department or call our on-call line if he develops diarrhea exceeding 4-6 loose stools per day above baseline, fever, severe abdominal pain, shortness of breath, or any other concerning new symptoms.

I reassured him that the intent of treatment remains to achieve meaningful tumor control and ideally a durable response. We discussed the data from CheckMate 067 showing that approximately 40-50% of patients derive long-term benefit from ipi/Opdivo combination, and that responses in melanoma can be deep and durable. His current performance status (ECOG 1), preserved organ function, and prior demonstrated immune response potential make him a reasonable candidate.

---

**PLAN**

1. **Discontinue dabra and tram effective today.** Both agents are being stopped given unequivocal disease progression. Drug holiday washout period of approximately 2 weeks prior to immunotherapy initiation to minimize risk of additive toxicities.

2. **Re-biopsy of new subcutaneous nodule on the right abdomen** -- core needle biopsy scheduled for this week with interventional radiology/procedure team. Tissue to be sent for:
   - Standard H&E and melanoma immunohistochemistry panel (S100, HMB-45, Melan-A, SOX10)
   - Ki-67 proliferation index
   - BRAF V600E IHC (VE1) to confirm retention
   - Comprehensive NGS panel (523-gene solid tumor panel, same platform as baseline) to characterize acquired resistance mechanism
   - Tissue banking for future research use (consent obtained)

3. **Brain MRI with and without gadolinium** -- ordered today, results to be reviewed prior to immunotherapy initiation. If CNS metastases identified, neurosurgery and/or radiation oncology consultation will be obtained urgently.

4. **Pre-immunotherapy baseline workup:**
   - Labs: CBC with differential, CMP, LFTs, LDH, TSH/free T4, AM cortisol, ACTH stimulation test, testosterone (in male patients), FSH/LH, Hgb A1c, urinalysis
   - ECG (baseline)
   - LVEF assessment by echocardiogram (given prior echo at baseline of 62% and prior targeted therapy; now establishing new baseline prior to immunotherapy)
   - Hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis B core antibody, hepatitis C antibody (immunotherapy clearance per institutional protocol)
   - ANA panel (screen for pre-existing autoimmune condition, though history is negative)

5. **Second-line systemic therapy -- Yervoy (Yervoy) + nivo (Opdivo):** Plan to initiate once brain MRI reviewed and confirmed negative for CNS disease, resistance biopsy obtained, and pre-treatment labs verified. Target start: approximately 2 weeks from today. Dosing: Yervoy 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 cycles in combination with nivo 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 cycles (CheckMate 067 dosing regimen), followed by nivo 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as single-agent maintenance. Tumor board review of this plan to be confirmed via e-consult prior to first infusion.

6. **Supportive care and monitoring:**
   - Patient education materials regarding immune-mediated adverse events provided and reviewed in detail
   - Stool diary to be maintained starting today
   - Written emergency contact information provided
   - Dermatology: continue monthly skin surveillance per current arrangement; advise dermatology of planned immunotherapy (potential for immune-mediated dermatological events, distinct from BRAF inhibitor-related cuSCC risk)
   - Nutritional assessment to be considered if LFTs or appetite worsen further

7. **Follow-up:**
   - Clinical appointment in 2 weeks (or sooner if new symptoms) to review brain MRI result, re-biopsy result (if available), baseline labs, and confirm immunotherapy start date
   - First infusion appointment to be scheduled by treatment coordination team

Patient verbalized understanding of the plan, the rationale for stopping the targeted therapy, and the rationale for transitioning to immunotherapy. He and his wife expressed appropriate comprehension and agreement. Questions answered at length. Time spent with patient today: approximately 65 minutes.

Addendum: If resistance biopsy NGS returns with a novel actionable finding (e.g., RET fusion, MET amplification, etc.) not present at baseline, we will reassess the therapeutic plan accordingly prior to immunotherapy initiation; however, this is not expected given the clinical context and known resistance mechanisms for BRAF-inhibitor-refractory melanoma.

[Electronically signed -- Attending Medical Oncologist]
[cc: Referring Physician, Dermatology, Interventional Radiology, Gastroenterology on file]
